data.title: As a performance engineer, ticket CLI latency regressions fail CI before they reach users
data.description:
## Description

**What**: Build a hyperfine-based regression test suite that measures per-call latency for every covered op and fails if SC1/SC2 regress by >10% against the A0 baseline. Add an aggregate-workflow benchmark that scripts a representative `/dso:sprint`-turn (20–40 ticket CLI calls with a ~50% read/write mix) and asserts ≥60% wall-clock reduction compared to the pre-refactor baseline. Capture per-platform SC1/SC2 targets with documented slack budgets for macOS and Alpine (where git-commit floor and the Alpine `python3 fcntl.flock` fallback make the reference Linux <0.05s target infeasible).

**Why**: SC6 and SC7 require a durable regression gate to keep the latency wins locked in. A one-off acceptance benchmark isn't enough — without a continuous gate, downstream changes could silently reintroduce Python processes or add overhead. Per-platform slack budgets prevent false-positive CI failures on non-reference hardware while still enforcing the reference targets.

**Scope**:
- IN: `hyperfine`-based per-op benchmark suite invoked via a single runner script; regression threshold of >10% vs A0 baseline triggers CI failure; aggregate `/dso:sprint`-turn benchmark script (20–40 calls, ~50% read/write mix, representative of real workflow use) with ≥60% reduction assertion; per-platform baselines and slack budgets committed alongside the A0 fixture (Linux reference = strict SC targets; macOS and Alpine = documented slack budgets); read-after-write consistency check integrated into the regression suite; existing `tests/scripts/test-ticket-subprocess-count.sh` passes unmodified; before/after results captured and ready to post as a closing comment on the epic.
- OUT: Cross-platform correctness testing (Story F); net-new library ops; documentation updates (Story H).

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, a hyperfine-based per-op regression suite runs in CI and fails if any covered op regresses by >10% against the A0 baseline
  ← Satisfies: "A new regression test suite measures per-call latency for each covered operation and fails if SC1/SC2 regress by >10%"
- When this story is complete, an aggregate-workflow benchmark script is checked in, runnable via one command, and asserts ≥60% aggregate wall-clock reduction for a representative `/dso:sprint`-turn (20–40 calls, ~50% read/write mix) versus the pre-refactor baseline
  ← Satisfies: "A representative /dso:sprint workflow trace — 20–40 ticket CLI calls with a ~50% read/write mix — demonstrates ≥60% aggregate ticket-CLI wall-clock reduction compared to the current dispatcher, measured end-to-end"
- When this story is complete, per-platform SC1/SC2 targets and slack budgets are committed alongside the A0 baseline fixture (Linux = strict targets; macOS and Alpine = documented slack) and the regression suite compares like-for-like
  ← Satisfies: "A new regression test suite measures per-call latency for each covered operation and fails if SC1/SC2 regress by >10%"
- When this story is complete, before/after benchmark results are captured in a format suitable for pasting as the epic's closing comment
  ← Satisfies: "The benchmark script is checked in, runnable via one command, and before/after results are captured in the closing comment"
- When this story is complete, the existing `tests/scripts/test-ticket-subprocess-count.sh` passes unmodified
  ← Satisfies: "All existing ticket-CLI test assertions (including tests/scripts/test-ticket-subprocess-count.sh) pass unmodified"
- When this story is complete, unit tests are written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations

- [Testing] CI runner variance (noisy-neighbor effects) handled via hyperfine's statistical outputs and a >10% threshold rather than absolute pass/fail
- [Performance] SC1 <0.05s target applies to Linux reference hardware; macOS and Alpine have documented slack budgets so git-commit intrinsic floor and Alpine's `python3 fcntl.flock` fallback do not produce false regression failures

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Proceed unless a significant assumption is required to continue — one that could send the implementation in the wrong direction. Escalate only when genuinely blocked without a reasonable inference. Document all assumptions made without escalating.
